This looks really nice, good idea putting the text in the middle of the line! Have you though about using the curses ACS_HLINE instead of just a dash? I think it would look a little cleaner to have a solid line across as opposed to a dashed line.

Two ways you could do it:
1. Use [window.hline](https://docs.python.org/2/library/curses.html#curses.window.hline) and just write the text on top of the line. Downside is that ``hline`` only draws white lines.
2. Do something similar to what I did to draw the vertical bars
```python
        for y in range(n_rows):
            x = 0
            # http://bugs.python.org/issue21088
            if (sys.version_info.major,
                sys.version_info.minor,
                sys.version_info.micro) == (3, 4, 0):
                x, y = y, x
            win.addch(y, x, curses.ACS_VLINE, attr)
```

Importing third party packages at the top of a file sometimes causes unexpected errors depending on how users install ChatterBot. The best way (that I have found) to avoid this is to protect the imports by placing them inside of a method as this prevents them from being imported when the file is loaded. I'm actually not certain why some people encounter the import errors but it seems that it may be the result of less conventional (but still somewhat valid) installation methods such as installing directly through `setup.py` commands.

This line -- "previous_versions=($(git tag --sort=-creatordate | grep --invert-match rc | head -n 5))" is version agnostic, which means even if you change the major version, the models will still be trained. 
With multiple minor versions, the training will happen on all of the tagged git versions. 

So essentially, whenever a new release (be it major version/minor version) comes out, it will be pulled in using the git-tag command and the training file will save a bunch of models for it in S3.

Django migrations can occasionally be buggy for the MySQL backend (more people use the Postgres backend as I understand it), so we tend to always check the output of `sqlmigrate` when making migrations to ensure it's doing the right thing.

Doing that now, I got:

```
vagrant ~/treeherder $ ./manage.py sqlmigrate model 0022
BEGIN;
--
-- Add field whiteboard to bugscache
--
ALTER TABLE `bugscache` ADD COLUMN `whiteboard` varchar(100) DEFAULT  NOT NULL;
ALTER TABLE `bugscache` ALTER COLUMN `whiteboard` DROP DEFAULT;
--
-- Alter field bug_id on bugjobmap
--
DROP INDEX `bug_job_map_bug_id_013db14b` ON `bug_job_map`;
--
-- Rename field bug_id on bugjobmap to bug
--
--
-- Alter unique_together for bugjobmap (1 constraint(s))
--
--
-- Alter field bug on bugjobmap
--
CREATE INDEX `bug_job_map_bug_id_013db14b` ON `bug_job_map` (`bug_id`);
ALTER TABLE `bug_job_map` ADD CONSTRAINT `bug_job_map_bug_id_013db14b_fk_bugscache_id` FOREIGN KEY (`bug_id`) REFERENCES `bugscache` (`id`);
--
-- Alter field time on push
--
CREATE INDEX `push_time_eb18a3b0` ON `push` (`time`);
COMMIT;
```

In it, the index on `bug_id` is being dropped and then recreated again. I believe this is because the field definition on this intermediate step is missing the `db_index=True`. 

If I change that line to this:
```python
models.PositiveIntegerField(db_index=True, db_column='bug_id'),
```

...then the generated migration looks fine (aside from the weird whiteboard `DEFAULT NOT NULL` followed immediately by `DROP DEFAULT` thing, but that must be a separate Django bug and not anything we need to worry about from a performance of applying the migration in production point of view):

```
BEGIN;
--
-- Add field whiteboard to bugscache
--
ALTER TABLE `bugscache` ADD COLUMN `whiteboard` varchar(100) DEFAULT  NOT NULL;
ALTER TABLE `bugscache` ALTER COLUMN `whiteboard` DROP DEFAULT;
--
-- Alter field bug_id on bugjobmap
--
--
-- Rename field bug_id on bugjobmap to bug
--
--
-- Alter unique_together for bugjobmap (1 constraint(s))
--
--
-- Alter field bug on bugjobmap
--
ALTER TABLE `bug_job_map` ADD CONSTRAINT `bug_job_map_bug_id_013db14b_fk_bugscache_id` FOREIGN KEY (`bug_id`) REFERENCES `bugscache` (`id`);
--
-- Alter field time on push
--
CREATE INDEX `push_time_eb18a3b0` ON `push` (`time`);
COMMIT;
```

```
'Usage:       fire/base_filename.py'
```
We can either use \s+ to be invariant to the number of spaces. Or, in the negative case, we can drop 'Usage:' altogether to assert that 'fire/base_filename' doesn't appear at all. Or (my preferred option) we can drop the negative case altogether, since the positive case already asserts that the full path isn't showing up in the usage section.

2. I think it's an awkward construction to put assertOutputMatches inside of an assertRaises. In step 2 "Create DIDs" multiple actions are done by calls of `utils.perform`. Each call of `utils.perform` here is done with `ignore_exception=True` which is used by default. So, for example, if creation of trustee1 fails then the current step status will be set to FAILED. But next if creation of trustee2 succeeds then the current step status is overwritten from FAILED to PASSED. Such the behavior is incorrect. Obviously, if some action in the step has failed then the whole step status must be FAILED.

To correct this behavior I propose to add every new step with PASSED status initially and don't update the step's status in `utils.perform` in case `func` has succeeded. With this logic we will get the step passed only if all the actions in it have succeed and we will get the whole step failed if any action in it has failed.

By the way, it is ok to add a new step with the initial PASSED status because if some error occurred not inside `utils.perform` call aborts a test case then the step's status will be set to FAILED in `except` section of `try` block in `TestScenarioBase.execute_scenario`.

`ret_flags_understood` is a set of return flags that are understood by the calling application.

In other word, it is a subset of possible `ret_flag` values from `gss_init/accept_sec_context`; the only useful `ret_flag_understood` parameter to set would be the channel bound flag; setting or not setting any other flag does not change the behavior. (This last point about not changing behavior is the subject of a revision in the draft, as if you set `req_flags = GSS_C_MUTUAL_FLAG` but set `ret_flags_understood = 0`, `gss_init/accept_sec_context` will still return GSS_C_MUTUAL_FLAG on success). 

`ret_flags` was what the original [draft](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-kitten-channel-bound-flag-01#section-2.2.1) called it in the C bindings, hence the discrepancy in the high level API. Suggestions welcome about a more helpful name.

Yes, the thing `Y` in a `for X in Y:` will be assigned to a temporary internal variable and have `iter()` called on it which then has `next()` called on it until it raises a `StopIteration` exception. In this case, `keys()` returns a `list` which which, when passed to `iter()` returns a `listiterator` object. Something like:
```python
for X in Y:
    ... something using X ...

→

_Y = iter(Y)
while True:
    try:
        X = next(_Y)
        ... something using X ...
    except StopIteration:
        break
```
so `Y` is only accessed once.
